items.q: What is reduction in terms of electrons?
items.correct: Gain of electrons (the oxidation number is reduced).
items.misconception: Reduction means losing oxygen and oxidation means gaining oxygen — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Reduction means a substance becomes physically smaller in size.
items.why: Students learn the oxygen definition first (from rusting/combustion) and never update to the more general electron-transfer definition.

items.q: Where is almost all the mass of an atom located?
items.correct: In the nucleus, made up of protons and neutrons.
items.misconception: Electrons contribute significantly to the mass of an atom.
items.random_wrong: Mass is spread evenly throughout the entire atom including the electron shells.
items.why: Students see electrons listed alongside protons and neutrons and assume they contribute comparable mass — in reality an electron has ~1/1836 the mass of a proton.

items.q: In a standard hydrogen electrode used as a reference, what is the assigned electrode potential?
items.correct: Exactly 0 V by convention — all other electrode potentials are measured relative to it.
items.misconception: The standard hydrogen electrode actually has zero potential because hydrogen has no charge.
items.random_wrong: The standard hydrogen electrode has a potential of -1 V to balance copper at +1 V.
items.why: Students don't realise 0 V is a chosen reference, not a measured value; they think hydrogen is genuinely neutral electrochemically.

items.q: If 25 g of product is obtained when 40 g is theoretical, % yield is:
items.why: Correct answer: 62.5%. “40%” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Addition. “Substitution” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, if you have 4 mol H₂ and 1 mol O₂, max mol H₂O made is:
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
